content.text: Claude Code plugins are versioned packages that bundle skills, hooks, MCP server configurations, and settings into a single installable unit. Where skills define individual slash commands and hooks react to lifecycle events, a plugin packages all of those extensions together so a team, organization, or community can share and reproduce a complete Claude Code setup with a single installation command.
A plugin is a directory (or npm package) that follows Claude Code's extension layout. Inside it, a
folder holds hook scripts, and a

file declares MCP server registrations, environment variables, and permission overrides. When Claude Code installs a plugin, it merges all of those contributions into its active configuration so every extension the plugin ships is immediately available.

directory (which is checked into a single repository and scoped to that repo), a plugin is designed to be shared across many projects and teams. It can be published to the npm registry, hosted in a git repository, or distributed as a local directory path. The key property of a plugin is that it is
— any developer who installs version 1.4.0 of a plugin gets exactly the same skills, hooks, and MCP configs as everyone else on that version.
Without plugins, every developer on a team either copies skill files into each repository's
folder — which leads to drift as the canonical version evolves — or configures hooks and MCP servers by hand in their
user directory, which is invisible to the rest of the team and impossible to audit or roll back. Both approaches break down as teams grow.
Plugins solve this with a single source of truth. The engineering platform team publishes a plugin called

and every application repository installs it. When the platform team fixes a bug in the deploy skill or adds a new MCP server, they bump the version and each team upgrades at their own pace — with a clear audit trail in
One command installs every skill, hook, and MCP server the plugin provides. New team members are productive immediately without a manual onboarding checklist.
The plugin version appears in configuration files under version control. Teams can pin to a stable release, review changelogs, and roll back if a new version introduces regressions.
Hooks that enforce security policies or coding standards apply identically across every repo that installs the plugin, not just repos that remember to copy the hook file.

A project can install a shared plugin and still define its own project-level skills and hooks. Plugin skills take priority in the search order, but project skills can override by name when a project needs a customised variant.

Open-source plugins on npm let the broader community share specialised workflows — language-specific linting hooks, cloud provider deploy skills, or security scanning MCP servers — without each team building from scratch.

Installing a plugin grants it the ability to register hooks that run arbitrary code during Claude Code sessions. A malicious or poorly reviewed plugin could intercept tool use or exfiltrate context. Only install plugins from sources you control or have audited.

When a plugin changes a skill's behavior, all repositories using that plugin are affected. Breaking changes in a hook that blocks tool use can halt every developer's workflow simultaneously, so the plugin author needs careful versioning discipline.
If two plugins both ship a skill called
, the one installed first wins (or the later entry in the search order wins, depending on load order). Plugins should namespace their skills to avoid this.

Publishing a plugin as an npm package adds a release cycle: semantic versioning, changelogs, testing across Claude Code versions. For a small team's internal tooling, project-level skills may be simpler to maintain.
When Claude Code installs a plugin, it resolves the package to a local directory (unpacking from npm, cloning from git, or symlinking a local path) and stores it under
. On startup, Claude Code scans all installed plugins and registers their contributions in this order:
is loaded as a skill. The filename (without
) becomes the slash command name. Plugin skills are added to the
of the search order, above project and user skills.
are merged with the active session's hook list. Each hook specifies the event, an optional tool matcher, and the path to the script inside the plugin directory.
entries in the plugin's settings are registered with Claude Code's MCP runtime, making the tools they expose available to Claude exactly like manually configured servers.
Permission overrides, environment variables, and model preferences declared by the plugin are merged with the local settings. Local settings take precedence over plugin-provided defaults.
Claude Code resolves a slash command by searching four tiers in order, returning the first match:

This ordering means a plugin can shadow a project skill if both define the same command name — which is useful when a plugin provides a stricter, organization-approved version of a common command. It can also be surprising if the shadowing is accidental, so plugin authors should prefer namespaced command names (e.g.,

The diagram below shows how a plugin package is structured, the three installation paths Claude Code supports, how installed plugins are resolved into the active session's skill search order, and the four categories of extensions a plugin can contribute.

content.text: Plugins are the right abstraction when the same extensions need to work across multiple projects or multiple developers. Use a plugin when:
A platform team wants to standardise deploy, lint, or test workflows across every service repository in an organisation.
Security hooks (e.g., blocking dangerous shell patterns or enforcing secret scanning) must apply uniformly and cannot rely on individual developers copying hook files.
An MCP server (database inspector, internal API browser, monitoring tool) is useful across projects but too complex to configure by hand each time.
An open-source library maintainer wants to publish a plugin so contributors get the same AI-assisted review and release workflows.
The skill is specific to a single repository. A project-level
file is simpler and shows up in the repository's version history directly.
The extension is personal preference only. User-level
skills are easier to manage without the overhead of a versioned package.
The team is small (2–3 developers) and copy-pasting a single skill file is genuinely simpler than maintaining a published package.
Start with an npm package that follows the plugin layout:

my-org-plugin/
├── package.json
├── .claude/
│   ├── commands/
│   │   ├── deploy.md
│   │   └── run-checks.md
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   └── pre-tool-lint.sh
│   └── settings.json
└── README.md
Step 2 — Add skills, hooks, and settings
Write each skill as a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter. Name the files with a namespace prefix to avoid collisions with project skills:

---
description: Deploy the app to a target environment (my-org standard workflow)
argument-hint: <environment>
---

You are running the my-org deploy workflow.
Target environment: $ARGUMENTS

1. Run `npm run build` and abort if it fails.
2. Validate environment name is one of: staging, production.
3. Run `./scripts/deploy.sh $ARGUMENTS` and stream the output.
4. Tail the health-check endpoint until it returns HTTP 200 or times out at 60s.
5. Report the deployment outcome and the URL of the deployed service.
Register hooks and MCP servers in the plugin's
. Paths to hook scripts should be relative to the plugin root:
Install from npm, a local path, or a git repository:

# From npm (published package)
claude plugin install @my-org/claude-plugin

# From a local directory (development)
claude plugin install ./my-org-plugin

# From a git repository
claude plugin install github:my-org/claude-plugin

# List installed plugins
claude plugin list

# Remove a plugin
claude plugin uninstall @my-org/claude-plugin
After installation, the plugin's skills are immediately available as slash commands. In the example above,
runs the deploy skill from the plugin.
A security team creates a plugin that intercepts all
tool calls and blocks commands known to exfiltrate credentials or delete data without confirmation:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/guard-bash.sh — blocks known-dangerous patterns
set -euo pipefail

input=$(cat)
cmd=$(printf '%s' "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')

# Block patterns that commonly exfiltrate secrets
dangerous_patterns=(
  'curl.*\$.*TOKEN'
  'wget.*\$.*SECRET'
  'env.*|.*curl'
  'cat.*\.env.*\|'
  'rm\s+-rf\s+/'
)

for pattern in "${dangerous_patterns[@]}"; do
  if echo "$cmd" | grep -qE "$pattern"; then
    printf '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Command matches security blocklist: %s"}}' "$pattern"
    exit 2
  fi
done

exit 0
The security team publishes this as
and mandates its installation in all developer onboarding scripts. Whenever Claude tries to run a flagged command, the hook blocks it and explains why.
A Python team creates a plugin that adds a
after Claude edits a Python file:

---
description: Review Python code for style, type safety, and common bugs
argument-hint: [file-or-path]
---

Review the Python code at $ARGUMENTS (or the current open file if not specified).

1. Run `ruff check $ARGUMENTS` and capture output.
2. Run `mypy $ARGUMENTS` and capture type errors.
3. Read the file and check for: missing docstrings on public APIs,
   mutable default arguments, bare except clauses, and N+1 query patterns.
4. Report all findings grouped by severity (error → warning → style).
5. Offer to apply auto-fixes for ruff-fixable issues.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/post-edit-ruff.sh — auto-lint Python files after Edit
input=$(cat)
tool=$(printf '%s' "$input" | jq -r '.tool_name')
path=$(printf '%s' "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')

if [[ "$tool" == "Edit" || "$tool" == "Write" ]] && [[ "$path" == *.py ]]; then
  ruff check --fix "$path" 2>&1 | head -20
fi
exit 0

A fintech company runs 40 microservices across six teams. Each service has its own repository, but all services share the same CI pipeline, the same deployment playbook, and the same internal observability tooling. Before plugins, each repository had its own copy of
that had drifted over 18 months into six different variations — some missing the health-check step, one still pointing at a deprecated rollback script.
The platform team consolidated everything into
— canonical deploy skill with environment validation, blue-green switchover, and smoke tests
— skill that fetches the current on-call runbook for a service from the internal wiki and presents it inline
commands without a matching incident ticket number in the session
An MCP server for the internal metrics platform so Claude can read Prometheus metrics inline during debugging
Each new service repository installs the plugin in its

onboarding script. When the platform team fixes a bug, they bump to 1.0.1 and send a Slack notification. Teams upgrade on their next sprint by changing one version number. The entire organisation moved from six divergent deploy scripts to one canonical one in a two-day migration.
A plugin that defines a skill named

skill because plugins take priority. If the project skill contains project-specific logic (a different S3 bucket name, a custom config path), the plugin version will silently override it. Always prefix plugin skills with an org or plugin identifier:

# Bad — collides with project and user skills
.claude/commands/deploy.md
.claude/commands/test.md

# Good — namespaced, avoids collisions
.claude/commands/acme-deploy.md
.claude/commands/acme-test.md
Hook scripts inside a plugin are stored at an install-time path under
in settings breaks when another developer installs the plugin. Always use paths relative to the plugin root or use environment variables that the plugin's settings inject.
Publishing without testing the installed form
Developers often test plugins from a local

where the working directory context matches the development setup. After publishing to npm, the package is unpacked into a different directory tree and relative paths or assumptions about the current working directory can break. Always test by installing from the published npm tarball before releasing.

hooks that intercept every tool call in every Claude Code session. A malicious plugin could read file contents or exfiltrate tool inputs over the network. Never install a plugin from an unvetted source, and review the hook scripts in
before installation, just as you would inspect any third-party script before executing it with shell access.
A hook behavior change that breaks existing workflows is a major version bump. Changing a skill's
is at most a minor. Developers pin to
and trust that minor/patch upgrades are safe.
hook runs synchronously before every matching tool call. A hook that takes 500 ms adds perceptible latency to every edit. Target under 50 ms for policy checks.

Each MCP server registered by a plugin starts a background process. Register only the servers genuinely needed across most projects; let individual repositories add project-specific MCP servers themselves.
list where only the description and argument-hint are visible. A vague description means the skill gets ignored.
Test against multiple Claude Code versions.

The hook event schema and skill resolution logic can change between Claude Code releases. Pin your CI test matrix to the Claude Code versions your users run, and add a CI step that installs the plugin fresh and verifies each skill and hook works as expected.

Plugins add startup overhead proportional to the number of extensions they register. On a typical developer machine, loading 10–20 skills and starting 1–2 MCP server processes adds under 200 ms to Claude Code's startup time. Beyond that, the main runtime cost comes from hooks.
hook spawns a subprocess for every matched tool call. If a plugin registers a hook that matches all

calls and Claude runs 50 bash commands in a session, the hook runs 50 times. Keep hook logic minimal — parse stdin, check a condition, exit immediately. Avoid shell scripts that spawn additional processes (e.g.,
inside a bash hook adds 30–80 ms interpreter startup per call). For complex validation logic, write the hook script in a compiled language or pre-warm a long-running process with

MCP servers added by plugins run as persistent background processes for the session's lifetime. Each server typically consumes 50–200 MB of RAM. Registering five unnecessary MCP servers from a plugin wastes 250 MB–1 GB of memory that persists until the session ends. Only bundle MCP servers that most plugin users will actually need.
The plugin lifecycle is managed through the
Install from npm, local path, or git repository
List all installed plugins with versions and skill counts
Upgrade a plugin to the latest compatible version
Remove a plugin and deregister all its extensions
Show all skills, hooks, and MCP servers a plugin provides
For publishing, use standard npm tooling:

# Publish to npm
npm publish --access public

# Or use a private registry
npm publish --registry https://npm.my-org.internal

# Test the installed form before publishing
npm pack
claude plugin install ./my-org-claude-plugin-1.0.0.tgz
The security surface of a plugin is wider than a single skill file because it can register hooks that execute on every tool call. Before installing any plugin, review these areas:
. Confirm they do not write context data to external endpoints, do not read files outside the project directory, and exit promptly. An exit-2 hook that silently runs
in the background is invisible to the user.
An MCP server listed in the plugin's
runs as a local process with your filesystem and network access. Verify the MCP server package's source and npm publish history just as you would any Node.js dependency.
A plugin is an npm package, which means it can have dependencies. Run
on the plugin before installation, and consider pinning to an exact version rather than a range to prevent a compromised transitive dependency from silently sliding in.
A hook that exits 2 on a broad matcher (e.g., all
calls) and has a bug in its condition logic can block all Claude Code output unexpectedly. Test hooks with edge-case inputs before deploying them via a plugin to every developer's machine.

For internal company plugins, prefer hosting on an internal npm registry with package signing enforced. Only allow package authors from a specific GitHub org to publish — this prevents npm account compromise from inserting a malicious hook into the organisation's standard developer setup.
Think of a Claude Code plugin as an
OS package for your AI assistant
drops CLI binaries, configuration files, and system hooks into a known location and makes them immediately available,
drops skills, hooks, and MCP servers into Claude Code and makes them immediately available in every session.
The analogy extends to the trust model: you trust
packages because they come from a curated, signed repository maintained by people you trust. You apply the same scrutiny to Claude Code plugins — the plugin that registers a
hook runs code in your shell on every tool invocation, which is the equivalent of a system-level daemon.
Skills inside the plugin are like the CLI tools the package installs: each one is a named, documented command that a human explicitly invokes. Hooks are like
modules: they react automatically to events, running without explicit invocation, and must therefore be held to a higher standard of review and performance.
A plugin bundles skills, hooks, MCP server configs, and settings into a single versioned, installable package — the unit of sharing for Claude Code extensions.
Plugins install from npm, a local directory, or a git URL and store their contents under
Plugin skills occupy the highest priority tier in the search order, above project, user, and built-in skills — intentional when enforcing policy, dangerous when causing accidental name collisions.
Use plugins when extensions need to span multiple repositories or multiple developers; use project skills for single-repo workflows; use user skills for personal shortcuts.
Namespace all skill file names in a plugin (e.g.,
) to avoid silently shadowing project or user skills with the same name.

Hooks registered by plugins run on every matching tool call in every session; keep them under 50 ms and audit their source carefully before installation, because they execute arbitrary shell code with your credentials.
Version plugins with semantic versioning discipline: breaking hook behavior or skill interface changes require a major version bump so teams can upgrade at their own pace.
